title: AWS IAM policy evaluation order — explicit Deny always wins
body:
IAM policy evaluation is not additive. An explicit Deny in any policy (identity, resource, SCP, permission boundary) overrides all Allows. This catches people who add a broad Allow but forget about existing Deny rules.

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "s3:*",
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
```

This looks like full S3 access, but it can be denied by:
1. An SCP at the org/OU level
2. A permissions boundary on the IAM entity
3. A resource-based policy with explicit Deny
4. A session policy (for assumed roles)

Debug with: `aws iam simulate-principal-policy --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123:user/dev --action-names s3:GetObject --resource-arns arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/*`

Or use the IAM Policy Simulator in the console. Also check CloudTrail for `AccessDenied` events with the `errorCode` field.

title: AWS Lambda cold starts vary wildly by runtime and memory setting
body:
Cold start latency depends on runtime, package size, memory allocation, and VPC configuration. Memory setting also affects CPU allocation proportionally.

| Runtime | Typical Cold Start | With VPC |
|---------|-------------------|----------|
| Node.js 20 | 100-300ms | +200ms |
| Python 3.12 | 150-400ms | +200ms |
| Java 21 | 1-5 seconds | +1-3s |
| .NET 8 | 400-800ms | +200ms |
| Rust/Go (AL2023) | 10-50ms | +200ms |

```yaml
# SAM template — optimize cold starts
Resources:
  MyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Runtime: nodejs20.x
      MemorySize: 1024  # More memory = more CPU = faster init
      Architectures: [arm64]  # Graviton: 20% cheaper, similar performance
      SnapStart:  # Java only — pre-initializes snapshot
        ApplyOn: PublishedVersions
      Environment:
        Variables:
          AWS_LAMBDA_EXEC_WRAPPER: /opt/bootstrap  # For extensions
```

Key optimizations: increase memory (1024MB+ gives a full vCPU), use arm64/Graviton, minimize package size, lazy-load SDK clients, use provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive functions.

title: AWS S3 presigned URLs expire based on credential type, not just expiry param
AWS CloudFormation stack updates can replace resources and cause data loss
body:
Certain property changes on CloudFormation resources trigger replacement (delete + create) rather than in-place update. This destroys data without warning.

```yaml
# Changing these properties REPLACES the resource:
Resources:
  MyDB:
    Type: AWS::RDS::DBInstance
    Properties:
      DBInstanceIdentifier: my-db  # Change this → REPLACEMENT (data loss!)
      DBName: myapp                # Change this → REPLACEMENT
      MasterUsername: admin         # Change this → REPLACEMENT
      Engine: postgres              # Change this → REPLACEMENT
      EngineVersion: '16.1'         # Change this → in-place update (safe)
      DBInstanceClass: db.t4g.medium  # Change this → some downtime, but safe
    DeletionPolicy: Retain  # CRITICAL: prevents deletion on replacement
    UpdateReplacePolicy: Retain
```

Always check the CloudFormation docs for "Update requires: Replacement" on any property you're changing. Use `DeletionPolicy: Retain` and `UpdateReplacePolicy: Retain` on stateful resources (databases, S3 buckets, EFS). Preview changes with `aws cloudformation create-change-set` before applying.

title: AWS VPC CIDR blocks can't overlap with peered VPCs or on-prem networks
body:
VPC peering, Transit Gateway, and VPN connections all require non-overlapping CIDR blocks. Using the default 172.31.0.0/16 everywhere makes peering impossible later.

```
# Bad: all VPCs use the same CIDR — can't peer
Prod VPC:    172.31.0.0/16
Staging VPC: 172.31.0.0/16
Dev VPC:     172.31.0.0/16

# Good: planned CIDR allocation
Prod VPC:    10.0.0.0/16    (10.0.0.0 - 10.0.255.255)
Staging VPC: 10.1.0.0/16    (10.1.0.0 - 10.1.255.255)
Dev VPC:     10.2.0.0/16    (10.2.0.0 - 10.2.255.255)
On-prem:     192.168.0.0/16 (no overlap with any VPC)
```

```typescript
// CDK — explicit CIDR planning
const vpc = new ec2.Vpc(this, 'ProdVpc', {
  ipAddresses: ec2.IpAddresses.cidr('10.0.0.0/16'),
  maxAzs: 3,
  subnetConfiguration: [
    { name: 'Public', subnetType: ec2.SubnetType.PUBLIC, cidrMask: 24 },
    { name: 'Private', subnetType: ec2.SubnetType.PRIVATE_WITH_EGRESS, cidrMask: 24 },
    { name: 'Isolated', subnetType: ec2.SubnetType.PRIVATE_ISOLATED, cidrMask: 24 },
  ],
});
```

Plan CIDR blocks before creating VPCs. You can add secondary CIDRs later, but you can't change the primary CIDR of an existing VPC.

title: AWS RDS automated backups are deleted when you delete the DB instance
body:
When you delete an RDS instance, all automated backups are deleted with it. Only manual snapshots survive instance deletion.

```bash
# Create manual snapshot before deleting
aws rds create-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier my-db \
  --db-snapshot-identifier my-db-final-snapshot

# Delete with final snapshot (recommended)
aws rds delete-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier my-db \
  --final-db-snapshot-identifier my-db-final-2024

# Delete WITHOUT snapshot (dangerous — no recovery)
aws rds delete-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier my-db \
  --skip-final-snapshot
```

For CloudFormation/CDK, always set `deletionProtection: true` and `removalPolicy: RETAIN` on production databases. The deletion protection requires a separate API call to disable before the instance can be deleted, providing a safety net.

Also note: RDS automated backups have a maximum retention of 35 days. For longer retention, set up automated manual snapshots via Lambda or AWS Backup.

title: AWS DynamoDB query requires partition key — you can't query by sort key alone
AWS SQS messages can be delivered more than once — design for idempotency
AWS API Gateway default timeout is 29 seconds and cannot be increased
body:
API Gateway REST APIs have a hard 29-second integration timeout. If your Lambda takes longer, the client gets a 504 Gateway Timeout even though Lambda continues executing.

```yaml
# SAM template — Lambda timeout independent of API Gateway
Resources:
  MyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Timeout: 900  # Lambda can run up to 15 minutes
      Events:
        Api:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /process
            Method: post
            # But API Gateway still times out at 29 seconds!
```

Solutions for long-running operations:

1. **Async pattern**: Return 202 Accepted immediately, process in background, poll for status
2. **Step Functions**: Orchestrate long workflows, return execution ARN to client
3. **WebSocket API**: Push result when ready (WebSocket API Gateway has 10-minute idle timeout)
4. **ALB**: Application Load Balancer supports up to 4000-second timeout for Lambda targets

```javascript
// Async pattern
export const handler = async (event) => {
  const jobId = crypto.randomUUID();
  await sqs.send(new SendMessageCommand({
    QueueUrl: process.env.QUEUE_URL,
    MessageBody: JSON.stringify({ jobId, ...JSON.parse(event.body) }),
  }));
  return { statusCode: 202, body: JSON.stringify({ jobId, status: 'processing' }) };
};
```

title: AWS Lambda environment variables are visible to anyone with GetFunctionConfiguration permission
AWS CloudWatch Logs default retention is infinite — costs grow silently
body:
CloudWatch Logs groups default to "Never expire" retention. Logs accumulate forever, and storage costs add up quickly at $0.03/GB/month.

```bash
# Check all log groups with no expiry
aws logs describe-log-groups \
  --query 'logGroups[?!retentionInDays].{name:logGroupName,size:storedBytes}' \
  --output table

# Set retention on existing log group
aws logs put-retention-policy \
  --log-group-name /aws/lambda/my-function \
  --retention-in-days 30

# Set retention for all Lambda log groups
for lg in $(aws logs describe-log-groups --log-group-name-prefix /aws/lambda/ --query 'logGroups[?!retentionInDays].logGroupName' --output text); do
  echo "Setting 30-day retention on $lg"
  aws logs put-retention-policy --log-group-name "$lg" --retention-in-days 30
done
```

In CDK, always set retention:
```typescript
new lambda.Function(this, 'MyFunc', {
  logRetention: logs.RetentionDays.ONE_MONTH,
  // ...
});
```

Common retention strategy: 7 days for dev, 30 days for staging, 90 days for production, archive to S3 for compliance.

title: AWS ECS Fargate tasks need explicit outbound internet access via NAT Gateway
AWS Secrets Manager charges per secret AND per API call
AWS cross-account access requires BOTH account policies to allow it
AWS EventBridge rules need resource-based policy on the target
AWS Lambda layers are extracted to /opt — path conflicts with runtime
body:
Lambda layers are extracted to `/opt` in the execution environment. Multiple layers can overwrite each other's files if they use the same paths.

```bash
# Layer structure must match the runtime's expected paths:
# Node.js — /opt/nodejs/node_modules/
# Python — /opt/python/lib/python3.12/site-packages/
# Binary — /opt/bin/

# Building a Node.js layer correctly:
mkdir -p layer/nodejs
cd layer/nodejs
npm init -y
npm install sharp
cd ..
zip -r layer.zip nodejs/

# The layer is extracted to:
# /opt/nodejs/node_modules/sharp/
# Node.js automatically adds /opt/nodejs/node_modules to NODE_PATH
```

Gotcha: if two layers both include the same npm package, the second layer overwrites the first. Layer order matters — listed from bottom (first extracted) to top (last extracted, wins conflicts).

For binaries, add `/opt/bin` to PATH (Lambda does this automatically). For shared libraries, add `/opt/lib` to `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`.

title: AWS CDK synth creates CloudFormation with auto-generated logical IDs that change on refactor
AWS Aurora Serverless v2 has a minimum capacity you pay for even when idle
AWS S3 bucket names are globally unique and can't be reclaimed after deletion
body:
S3 bucket names are unique across ALL AWS accounts worldwide. If you delete a bucket, someone else could claim the name. Also, bucket names appear in URLs, so they're effectively public information.

```bash
# This bucket name is gone forever if someone else takes it
aws s3 rb s3://my-company-production-data --force

# Use account-specific naming conventions
aws s3 mb s3://123456789012-us-east-1-myapp-data
# Or: s3://mycompany-prod-us-east-1-data
```

Security implications:
1. Old S3 URLs in code, docs, or logs reveal your bucket names
2. Deleted buckets can be re-created by attackers (subdomain takeover)
3. If your app references a deleted bucket, an attacker could serve malicious content

Best practice: use predictable naming conventions (account ID + region + purpose), never delete production buckets (empty them instead), and use bucket policies to restrict access by VPC or IP.

title: AWS Lambda execution context reuse means global state persists between invocations
AWS IAM roles for Lambda need both execution role and resource policies
AWS DynamoDB on-demand pricing can be 5-7x more expensive than provisioned at steady state
AWS NAT Gateway data processing charges apply in both directions
body:
NAT Gateway charges $0.045/GB for data processed — and it's charged for both outbound internet access AND cross-AZ traffic within your VPC.

```
Data flow costs through NAT Gateway:

Internet access:
  Private subnet → NAT GW → Internet = $0.045/GB processing + data transfer

Cross-AZ (surprise!):
  Private subnet AZ-a → NAT GW AZ-a → Service in AZ-b = $0.045/GB
  (Plus $0.01/GB cross-AZ transfer)

Same-AZ to S3:
  Private subnet → NAT GW → S3 = $0.045/GB
  Private subnet → S3 VPC endpoint → S3 = FREE
```

Cost reduction strategies:
1. **S3 Gateway Endpoint**: Free, eliminates NAT for S3 traffic
2. **DynamoDB Gateway Endpoint**: Free, same for DynamoDB
3. **Interface Endpoints**: For ECR, CloudWatch, SSM — $7.20/month per AZ but no per-GB charge
4. **One NAT per AZ vs shared**: Shared NAT saves $32/month per AZ but adds cross-AZ costs

For a typical workload doing 1TB/month through NAT Gateway, that's $45 in processing alone. Gateway endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB are always worth adding.

title: AWS SNS message filtering reduces Lambda invocations but has syntax gotchas
AWS Lambda provisioned concurrency doesn't eliminate cold starts — it front-loads them
AWS CDK bootstrap must be run in every account AND region you deploy to
body:
CDK bootstrap creates an S3 bucket and IAM roles needed for deployment. You must bootstrap each target account+region combination separately.

```bash
# Bootstrap the current account in us-east-1
cdk bootstrap aws://123456789012/us-east-1

# Bootstrap multiple environments
cdk bootstrap aws://123456789012/us-east-1 aws://123456789012/eu-west-1

# Cross-account bootstrap (for CI/CD pipeline)
cdk bootstrap aws://999999999999/us-east-1 \
  --trust 123456789012 \
  --cloudformation-execution-policies 'arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess'

# Upgrade existing bootstrap stack
cdk bootstrap --force
```

Common errors:
- `Need to perform AWS calls for account X, but no credentials found` → Wrong AWS profile
- `Policy contains a statement with one or more invalid principals` → Target account doesn't trust the source account
- `This CDK CLI is not compatible with the CDK library used by your application` → Version mismatch between CLI and CDK library

The bootstrap stack (`CDKToolkit`) can be customized for security requirements (encryption, permissions boundary).

title: AWS Lambda function URLs have no built-in authorization — anyone can call them
AWS ECS task definition revisions are immutable — updates create new revisions
body:
You can't modify an existing ECS task definition. Every change creates a new revision number. Old revisions persist and count against your account limits.

```bash
# Register new revision (creates family:2, family:3, etc.)
aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://task-def.json

# Update service to use new revision
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster my-cluster \
  --service my-service \
  --task-definition my-task:3

# Clean up old revisions (they accumulate!)
for arn in $(aws ecs list-task-definitions --family my-task --status ACTIVE --query 'taskDefinitionArns[:-3]' --output text); do
  aws ecs deregister-task-definition --task-definition $arn
done
```

Gotcha: each CI/CD deployment creates a new revision. After 1000 deployments, you have 1000 revisions. Deregister old ones to avoid hitting the limit and cluttering the console. CDK and Terraform create new revisions on every deploy even if nothing changed.

title: AWS S3 eventual consistency is gone but LIST operations have a caveat
AWS Lambda error: Task timed out after X seconds — common causes and fixes
AWS Lambda error: ENOMEM — out of memory crash with exit code 137
AWS CloudFormation error: UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED — stuck stack recovery
body:
When a stack update fails and the rollback also fails, the stack enters UPDATE_ROLLBACK_FAILED state. You can't update, delete, or do anything with it normally.

```bash
# Check which resources failed
aws cloudformation describe-stack-events \
  --stack-name my-stack \
  --query 'StackEvents[?ResourceStatus==`UPDATE_FAILED`].[LogicalResourceId,ResourceStatusReason]' \
  --output table

# Continue rollback, skipping the problematic resources
aws cloudformation continue-update-rollback \
  --stack-name my-stack \
  --resources-to-skip MyBrokenResource AnotherBrokenResource

# If that still fails, try with a different role
aws cloudformation continue-update-rollback \
  --stack-name my-stack \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminCfnRole
```

Common causes:
- Manually deleted a resource that CloudFormation manages
- IAM permission was removed during update
- Resource in a failed state that can't be rolled back

Prevention: never manually modify CloudFormation-managed resources. Use `DeletionPolicy: Retain` on critical resources. Enable termination protection on production stacks.

title: AWS API Gateway error: Execution failed due to configuration error — invalid mapping
body:
This generic error hides several specific issues. Enable API Gateway execution logging to see the real error.

```bash
# Enable execution logging
aws apigateway update-stage \
  --rest-api-id abc123 \
  --stage-name prod \
  --patch-operations \
  op=replace,path=/*/logging/loglevel,value=INFO

# Check logs in CloudWatch
# Log group: API-Gateway-Execution-Logs_{rest-api-id}/{stage}
```

Common causes of "Execution failed due to configuration error":

1. **Lambda permission missing**: API Gateway can't invoke the Lambda
```bash
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name my-func \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal apigateway.amazonaws.com \
  --source-arn "arn:aws:execute-api:us-east-1:123:abc123/*/GET/users"
```

2. **Response mapping template error**: VTL template syntax error
3. **Integration response not configured**: Missing status code mapping
4. **Timeout**: Backend took >29 seconds

The most common is #1 — especially after deleting and recreating a Lambda function (the permission doesn't survive recreation).

title: AWS DynamoDB error: ProvisionedThroughputExceededException — throttling
AWS ECS error: service unable to place task — insufficient resources
body:
ECS can't place a task when no container instance has enough CPU, memory, or ports available. With Fargate, it's usually a capacity provider issue.

```bash
# Check why tasks can't be placed
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster my-cluster \
  --services my-service \
  --query 'services[0].events[:5]'

# Common errors:
# "service my-service was unable to place a task because no container instance 
#  met all of its requirements"
```

EC2 launch type fixes:
1. Add more instances to the cluster
2. Reduce task CPU/memory requirements
3. Check placement constraints aren't too restrictive
4. Ensure container instance has required ports available

Fargate fixes:
1. Check the AZ has Fargate capacity (rare but happens)
2. Verify subnet has available IP addresses
3. Check security group allows outbound to ECR/S3 for image pulls
4. Ensure platform version is correct: `LATEST` vs specific version

```yaml
# ECS service with spread placement to avoid single-AZ issues
PlacementStrategies:
  - Type: spread
    Field: attribute:ecs.availability-zone
```

title: AWS Lambda error: ENOSPC — /tmp directory full at 512MB
AWS CDK error: Cannot find asset — bundling failures during cdk synth
AWS SQS error: messages reappear after processing — visibility timeout too short
AWS S3 error: Access Denied on GetObject despite bucket policy allowing it
body:
S3 Access Denied is the most frustrating AWS error because multiple layers of permissions can deny access, and the error message doesn't tell you which one.

```
Debug checklist (in evaluation order):

1. S3 Block Public Access settings (account + bucket level)
   aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket my-bucket

2. Bucket policy
   aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket my-bucket

3. IAM identity policy (user/role)
   aws iam simulate-principal-policy ...

4. VPC endpoint policy (if accessing from VPC)
   aws ec2 describe-vpc-endpoints --filters Name=service-name,Values=com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3

5. KMS key policy (if bucket uses SSE-KMS)
   aws kms describe-key --key-id <key-id>
   aws kms get-key-policy --key-id <key-id> --policy-name default

6. Object ownership (if cross-account upload without bucket-owner-full-control)
   aws s3api get-object-acl --bucket my-bucket --key my-file

7. SCPs (organization-level deny)
   Check AWS Organizations console
```

Most common cause: KMS key policy doesn't grant `kms:Decrypt` to the accessing principal. Second most common: the object was uploaded by another account without `--acl bucket-owner-full-control`.

title: AWS Lambda error: Runtime.ImportModuleError — can't find handler module
body:
The most common Lambda deployment error. The handler path doesn't match the actual file structure in the deployment package.

```
Runtime.ImportModuleError: Error: Cannot find module 'index'
```

```bash
# Handler format: <file>.<function>
# If handler is set to 'index.handler', Lambda looks for:
# /var/task/index.js (or index.mjs) → exports.handler

# Common mistakes:
# 1. File is in a subdirectory
#    Handler: index.handler
#    Actual:  src/index.js → Set handler to: src/index.handler

# 2. Zip file has an extra directory level
#    Expected: zip contains index.js at root
#    Actual: zip contains myapp/index.js

# Verify zip structure:
unzip -l deployment.zip
# Should show:
#   index.js
#   node_modules/
# NOT:
#   myapp/index.js
#   myapp/node_modules/

# 3. ESM module without .mjs extension or package.json type
#    If using import/export, either:
#    - Rename to .mjs and set handler to: index.handler
#    - Add "type": "module" to package.json

# 4. TypeScript not compiled
#    Lambda runs .js, not .ts — need a build step
```

Debug: download the deployed package from the Lambda console or `aws lambda get-function --function-name my-func` and inspect the zip structure.

title: AWS CloudFormation error: circular dependency between resources
body:
CloudFormation can't resolve resource creation order when two resources reference each other. This is especially common with security groups and Lambda permissions.

```yaml
# Circular dependency: SG-A references SG-B, SG-B references SG-A
Resources:
  # This creates a circular dependency
  SecurityGroupA:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
    Properties:
      SecurityGroupIngress:
        - SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupB  # References B

  SecurityGroupB:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
    Properties:
      SecurityGroupIngress:
        - SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupA  # References A

  # Fix: Use separate ingress rules
  SecurityGroupA:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup

  SecurityGroupB:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup

  IngressAFromB:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroupIngress
    Properties:
      GroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupA
      SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupB
      IpProtocol: tcp
      FromPort: 443
      ToPort: 443

  IngressBFromA:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroupIngress
    Properties:
      GroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupB
      SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref SecurityGroupA
      IpProtocol: tcp
      FromPort: 443
      ToPort: 443
```

The fix is to extract the cross-references into separate resources, breaking the cycle.

title: AWS RDS error: too many connections — connection pool exhaustion
AWS Lambda error: function invocation failed with 429 TooManyRequestsException
AWS CDK error: Insufficient permissions to deploy — bootstrap version mismatch
body:
CDK deployments fail with permission errors when the bootstrap stack is outdated. CDK v2 requires bootstrap version 6+.

```bash
# Check current bootstrap version
aws cloudformation describe-stacks \
  --stack-name CDKToolkit \
  --query 'Stacks[0].Outputs[?OutputKey==`BootstrapVersion`].OutputValue'

# Error: This CDK deployment requires bootstrap stack version '21',
# but the bootstrap stack 'CDKToolkit' has version '6'

# Fix: upgrade bootstrap
cdk bootstrap

# If using custom bootstrap template:
cdk bootstrap --template bootstrap-template.yaml

# Verify
cdk doctor
```

Other common CDK deployment errors:
- `User: arn:... is not authorized to perform: cloudformation:CreateChangeSet` → IAM permission missing
- `Resource handler returned message: "Rate exceeded"` → Too many API calls, add retries
- `Template error: instance of Fn::Select references undefined index` → Conditional resource with wrong index

Always run `cdk diff` before `cdk deploy` to catch issues early.

title: AWS VPC error: no available IP addresses in subnet
body:
Subnets run out of IP addresses when the CIDR is too small or ENIs from Lambda, ECS, or ELBs aren't cleaned up properly.

```bash
# Check available IPs in a subnet
aws ec2 describe-subnets \
  --subnet-ids subnet-abc123 \
  --query 'Subnets[0].AvailableIpAddressCount'

# A /24 subnet has 251 usable IPs (256 - 5 reserved by AWS)
# Lambda in VPC uses 1 ENI per concurrent execution (with Hyperplane, fewer)
# ECS Fargate uses 1 IP per task

# Find ENIs consuming IPs
aws ec2 describe-network-interfaces \
  --filters Name=subnet-id,Values=subnet-abc123 \
  --query 'NetworkInterfaces[].{Id:NetworkInterfaceId,Type:InterfaceType,Description:Description}'
```

Fixes:
1. Use larger subnets (/20 = 4091 IPs instead of /24 = 251)
2. Add secondary CIDR to VPC and create new subnets
3. Clean up orphaned ENIs from deleted Lambda/ECS resources
4. For Lambda: use VPC Hyperplane (automatic since 2019) which shares ENIs across functions

Plan for growth: `/24` per subnet is often too small for production. Use `/20` for private subnets and `/24` for public subnets.

title: AWS SQS error: message stuck in dead letter queue — debugging DLQ messages
AWS Lambda error: Unhandled Promise Rejection crashes Node.js 18+ runtime
AWS ECS error: CannotPullContainerError — ECR image pull failures
body:
ECS tasks fail to start because they can't pull the container image. This has multiple causes depending on your network setup.

```
CannotPullContainerError: Error response from daemon: pull access denied
CannotPullContainerError: Error response from daemon: Get https://xxx.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v2/: net/http: request canceled
```

Checklist:

```bash
# 1. Task execution role needs ECR permissions
# Attach AmazonECSTaskExecutionRolePolicy or:
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": [
    "ecr:GetAuthorizationToken",
    "ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability",
    "ecr:GetDownloadUrlForLayer",
    "ecr:BatchGetImage"
  ],
  "Resource": "*"
}

# 2. Network connectivity (Fargate in private subnet)
# Need NAT Gateway OR VPC endpoints for:
# - com.amazonaws.region.ecr.dkr
# - com.amazonaws.region.ecr.api
# - com.amazonaws.region.s3 (ECR stores layers in S3)

# 3. Security group allows outbound HTTPS (443)

# 4. Image URI is correct
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name my-app --image-ids imageTag=latest

# 5. ECR repository policy allows cross-account access (if applicable)
aws ecr get-repository-policy --repository-name my-app
```

The most common cause is missing VPC endpoints or NAT Gateway for Fargate tasks in private subnets.

title: AWS DynamoDB single-table design pattern for related entities
AWS Lambda Powertools for structured logging, tracing, and metrics
AWS Step Functions for long-running workflows with retry and error handling
AWS cost optimization pattern — tagging strategy for cost allocation
AWS EventBridge event-driven architecture pattern
AWS Lambda middleware pattern with middy for cross-cutting concerns
AWS multi-account organization pattern for production workloads
body:
AWS best practice is separate accounts for different environments and workloads, managed via AWS Organizations.

```
Organization Root
├── Management Account (billing, org policies only)
├── OU: Security
│   ├── Log Archive Account (centralized CloudTrail, Config)
│   └── Security Tooling Account (GuardDuty, Security Hub)
├── OU: Infrastructure
│   ├── Shared Services Account (CI/CD, VPN, DNS)
│   └── Networking Account (Transit Gateway, Direct Connect)
├── OU: Workloads
│   ├── OU: Production
│   │   ├── App1 Prod Account
│   │   └── App2 Prod Account
│   └── OU: Non-Production
│       ├── App1 Dev/Staging Account
│       └── App2 Dev/Staging Account
└── OU: Sandbox
    └── Developer Sandbox Accounts
```

Key SCPs per OU:
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "DenyRootUser",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "*",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": { "aws:PrincipalArn": "arn:aws:iam::*:root" }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyRegionsExceptApproved",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "NotAction": ["iam:*", "sts:*", "organizations:*"],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringNotEquals": {
          "aws:RequestedRegion": ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Use AWS Control Tower to automate this setup. Even solo founders should use at least 3 accounts: management, production, development.

title: AWS S3 event-driven pipeline for file processing
AWS CloudFront with S3 origin using OAC for secure static hosting
AWS Lambda function composition with SQS for reliable async processing
AWS infrastructure testing pattern with CDK assertions
AWS Lambda response streaming for large payloads and time-to-first-byte
AWS least-privilege IAM pattern using CDK grant methods
AWS SAM template for production Lambda API with custom domain
body:
A complete SAM template for a production API with Lambda, DynamoDB, and custom domain.

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31

Globals:
  Function:
    Runtime: nodejs20.x
    MemorySize: 1024
    Timeout: 10
    Architectures: [arm64]
    Tracing: Active
    Environment:
      Variables:
        TABLE_NAME: !Ref OrdersTable
        LOG_LEVEL: INFO
    Layers:
      - !Sub arn:aws:lambda:${AWS::Region}:094274105915:layer:AWSLambdaPowertoolsTypeScriptV2:1

Resources:
  Api:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::HttpApi
    Properties:
      StageName: prod
      Domain:
        DomainName: api.example.com
        CertificateArn: !Ref Certificate
        Route53:
          HostedZoneId: Z1234567890
      CorsConfiguration:
        AllowOrigins: ['https://example.com']
        AllowMethods: [GET, POST, PUT, DELETE]
        AllowHeaders: [Authorization, Content-Type]

  CreateOrderFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: src/handlers/create-order.handler
      Events:
        Api:
          Type: HttpApi
          Properties:
            ApiId: !Ref Api
            Path: /orders
            Method: POST
      Policies:
        - DynamoDBCrudPolicy:
            TableName: !Ref OrdersTable
    Metadata:
      BuildMethod: esbuild
      BuildProperties:
        Minify: true
        Target: es2022
        EntryPoints: [src/handlers/create-order.ts]

  OrdersTable:
    Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
    DeletionPolicy: Retain
    Properties:
      BillingMode: PAY_PER_REQUEST
      KeySchema:
        - { AttributeName: PK, KeyType: HASH }
        - { AttributeName: SK, KeyType: RANGE }
      AttributeDefinitions:
        - { AttributeName: PK, AttributeType: S }
        - { AttributeName: SK, AttributeType: S }
      PointInTimeRecoverySpecification:
        PointInTimeRecoveryEnabled: true
```

Deploy with `sam build && sam deploy --guided`. SAM's `esbuild` metadata enables fast TypeScript bundling without Docker.

title: AWS CDK best practices — project structure and configuration
AWS Lambda function configuration for optimal cold start performance
AWS CloudWatch alarms and dashboard configuration for Lambda monitoring
AWS IAM policy for least-privilege CI/CD pipeline deployments
body:
A CI/CD pipeline IAM role should have just enough permissions to deploy, not AdministratorAccess.

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "CloudFormationDeployment",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "cloudformation:CreateStack",
        "cloudformation:UpdateStack",
        "cloudformation:DeleteStack",
        "cloudformation:DescribeStacks",
        "cloudformation:DescribeStackEvents",
        "cloudformation:GetTemplate",
        "cloudformation:CreateChangeSet",
        "cloudformation:ExecuteChangeSet",
        "cloudformation:DeleteChangeSet",
        "cloudformation:DescribeChangeSet"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:cloudformation:us-east-1:123456789012:stack/myapp-*/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CDKAssets",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:ListBucket"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::cdk-*-assets-123456789012-us-east-1",
        "arn:aws:s3:::cdk-*-assets-123456789012-us-east-1/*"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CDKLookup",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ssm:GetParameter"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:ssm:us-east-1:123456789012:parameter/cdk-bootstrap/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AssumeDeployRole",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cdk-*-deploy-role-*",
        "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cdk-*-file-publishing-role-*",
        "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cdk-*-image-publishing-role-*",
        "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/cdk-*-lookup-role-*"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

CDK uses separate roles for deployment, asset publishing, and lookup. The CI/CD pipeline only needs `sts:AssumeRole` for these CDK bootstrap roles. The actual resource creation permissions are on the CloudFormation execution role, which is managed by the bootstrap.

AWS Lambda vs ECS Fargate — when to use each for APIs
body:
Both Lambda and Fargate are serverless compute, but they optimize for different workloads.

| Factor | Lambda | ECS Fargate |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| Cold start | 100-500ms (Node.js) | None (always running) |
| Max request duration | 15 minutes | Unlimited |
| Max memory | 10 GB | 120 GB |
| Pricing model | Per-invocation + duration | Per-second (CPU + memory) |
| Concurrency | 1000 default (soft limit) | Task count |
| Scaling speed | Milliseconds | Minutes |
| Persistent connections | No (WebSocket via API GW) | Yes (WebSocket, gRPC) |
| Container support | Container images up to 10GB | Any container |
| Cost at 1M req/month | ~$20 | ~$70 (2 tasks) |
| Cost at 100M req/month | ~$2000 | ~$140 (4 tasks with autoscaling) |

**Choose Lambda when:**
- Traffic is spiky or unpredictable
- Requests complete in under 29 seconds (API Gateway limit)
- You want zero operational overhead
- Cost optimization for low to medium traffic

**Choose Fargate when:**
- Consistent high traffic (cheaper at scale)
- Need persistent connections (WebSocket, gRPC, SSE)
- Requests take >29 seconds
- Need more than 10GB memory
- Running existing Docker containers without modification

Hybrid: Use Lambda for event-driven background tasks and Fargate for the main API.

title: AWS DynamoDB vs Aurora Serverless vs RDS — database selection guide
body:
Choosing the right AWS database depends on your access patterns, consistency needs, and scale.

| Factor | DynamoDB | Aurora Serverless v2 | RDS PostgreSQL |
|--------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Data model | Key-value / document | Relational (SQL) | Relational (SQL) |
| Scaling | Automatic, unlimited | Auto (0.5-128 ACU) | Manual (instance resize) |
| Min cost | $0 (on-demand, no traffic) | ~$43/month (0.5 ACU) | ~$13/month (db.t4g.micro) |
| Max throughput | Millions req/sec | ~100k queries/sec | Instance-dependent |
| Latency | Single-digit ms | Low ms | Low ms |
| Transactions | Yes (25 items max) | Full ACID | Full ACID |
| Joins | No | Yes | Yes |
| Schema flexibility | Schemaless | Schema required | Schema required |
| Backup/PITR | Built-in | Built-in | Built-in |
| Lambda integration | Native (streams, direct) | Via RDS Proxy | Via RDS Proxy |

**Choose DynamoDB when:**
- Known access patterns (can model as key-value)
- Need infinite scale with consistent performance
- Event-driven architecture (DynamoDB Streams)
- Key-value or simple document lookups

**Choose Aurora Serverless when:**
- Complex queries, joins, aggregations
- Variable traffic with auto-scaling needs
- Migration from existing MySQL/PostgreSQL
- Need full SQL flexibility

**Choose RDS when:**
- Predictable traffic (fixed instance is cheapest)
- Need specific PostgreSQL/MySQL extensions
- Budget-sensitive with steady workload

title: AWS CloudFormation vs CDK vs Terraform — IaC tool comparison
body:
Choosing an Infrastructure as Code tool for AWS has long-term implications for your team.

| Factor | CloudFormation | CDK | Terraform |
|--------|---------------|-----|----------|
| Language | YAML/JSON | TypeScript, Python, Go | HCL |
| State management | AWS-managed (stack) | AWS-managed (via CFN) | Self-managed (S3 + DynamoDB) |
| Multi-cloud | No | No | Yes |
| Preview changes | Change sets | `cdk diff` | `terraform plan` |
| Speed of updates | Slow (serial) | Slow (generates CFN) | Faster (parallel) |
| Abstraction level | Low (1:1 resources) | High (constructs, patterns) | Medium |
| Learning curve | Medium | Low (if you know TypeScript) | Medium |
| Ecosystem | AWS-only | Construct Hub | Registry (providers) |
| Drift detection | Drift detection API | Via CFN | `terraform refresh` |

**Choose CDK when:**
- AWS-only infrastructure
- TypeScript/Python team
- Want high-level abstractions (L2/L3 constructs)
- Rapid prototyping with sensible defaults

**Choose Terraform when:**
- Multi-cloud (AWS + GCP + Azure)
- Team already knows HCL
- Need faster deployment cycles
- Want mature state management and workspace features

**Choose CloudFormation when:**
- Simple infrastructure, minimal resources
- Need AWS-native drift detection
- Already have existing CFN templates

For most AWS-only teams starting fresh, CDK in TypeScript offers the best developer experience with the least boilerplate.

title: AWS API Gateway REST vs HTTP vs Lambda Function URL — choosing the right API type
body:
AWS offers three ways to expose Lambda functions as HTTP APIs, each with different features and pricing.

| Feature | REST API | HTTP API | Function URL |
|---------|----------|----------|-------------|
| Price (per million) | $3.50 | $1.00 | Free (Lambda cost only) |
| Latency overhead | ~15ms | ~5ms | ~2ms |
| WebSocket | Separate product | No | No |
| Request validation | Yes (models) | No | No |
| Usage plans/API keys | Yes | No | No |
| WAF integration | Yes | No | No |
| Custom authorizers | Lambda, Cognito | Lambda, JWT, Cognito | None (DIY) |
| Response caching | Yes | No | No |
| Request/response transform | VTL templates | Parameter mapping | No |
| Custom domain | Yes | Yes | No (CloudFront needed) |
| Max timeout | 29s | 29s | 15 min (streaming) |
| Max payload | 10 MB | 10 MB | 6 MB (20 MB streaming) |

**Choose REST API when:** Need WAF, caching, request validation, API keys, or usage plans.

**Choose HTTP API when:** Want lower cost, lower latency, JWT auth — and don't need REST API features.

**Choose Function URL when:** Internal tools, webhooks, prototypes. Zero infrastructure to manage, but no built-in auth, rate limiting, or WAF. Combine with CloudFront for custom domains and caching.

For most new projects, start with HTTP API. Migrate to REST API if you need its advanced features.
